claim: In the Ni-free sample, the calculated elastic properties of κ-carbides were compared with those of the B2 intermetallic Fe3Al-L12.
evidence_text: In the Ni-free sample, the calculated elastic properties of κ-carbides were compared with those of the B2 intermetallic Fe3Al-L12

claim: Increasing the annealing temperature led to significant improvement of the strength-ductility balance in both grades due to the removal of κ-carbides and the formation of nano-sized B2 disks in the ferrite phase.
evidence_text: Increasing the annealing temperature led to signifcant improvement of the strength-ductility balance in both the grades due to the removal of κ-carbides and the formation of nano-sized B2 disks in the ferrite phase.

claim: At 900–1050 °C, B2 formed as micron-sized particles and grain boundary precipitates in austenite and as nano-sized discs in ferrite; the discs were Ni-Al type in Ni-containing samples and Fe-Al type in Ni-free samples.
evidence_text: For both the grades at temperatures from 900 to 1050°C… B2 formed as micron-sized particles and grain boundary precipitates in the austenite phase and as nano-sized discs in the ferrite (see Fig. 1 (blue circle) and Fig. 2b and e). Te discs were of Ni-Al type in the Ni-containing samples but of Fe-Al type in Ni-free samples.

claim: At 500–700 °C, κ-carbides formed nano-sized blocks in austenite in both Ni-containing and Ni-free samples, and as micron-sized needles within ferrite only in the Ni-free sample.
evidence_text: At temperatures from 500 to 700°C, κ-carbides formed nano-sized blocks in the austenite phase in both Ni-containing and Ni-free samples and as micron-sized needles within the ferrite only in Ni-free sample (Fig. 1 (red circle) and Fig. 2b and f).

claim: An increase in the Mn content of the κ-phase significantly increases the strength of the bulk material because weaker Fe-C bonds are substituted by stronger Mn-C bonds.
evidence_text: an increase in the Mn content of the κ-phase can signifcantly increase the strength of the bulk material. Tis efect was attributed to the substitution of weaker Fe-C bonds with stronger Mn-C bonds.

claim: The experimental alloys were solution heat-treated at 1250 °C for 35 min in argon and water quenched.
evidence_text: Te experimental alloys were melted in an induction furnace, solution heat-treated for 35 min at 1250°C in a protective argon atmosphere and then water quenched.

claim: Annealing treatments were conducted at 500 °C, 700 °C, 900 °C, and 1050 °C.
evidence_text: Te annealing treatments were conducted at 500°C, 700°C, 900°C., and 1050°C.

claim: The highest annealing temperature of 1050 °C was selected to study the change in nanomechanical properties arising from an increase in the Ni and Al content of the nanosized disklike B2 particles.
evidence_text: Te highest annealing temperature of 1050°C was selected to study the change in the nanomechanical properties arising from an increase in the Ni and Al content of the nanosized disklike B2 particles.

claim: A microstructure similar to that formed at 500 °C was observed after isothermal holding at 700 °C.
evidence_text: (c) and (d) Similar microstructure to the one formed during annealing at 500°C was observed afer isothermal holding at 700°C.

claim: Disk-like B2 particles formed in the α matrix after annealing at 900 °C and 1050 °C.
evidence_text: (e) TEM-bright feld image of a disk-like B2 particle in the α matrix afer annealing at higher temperatures (e.g. 900°C and 1050°C).

claim: Cuboid κ particles formed in the γ matrix.
evidence_text: (f) TEM-bright feld image of cuboid κ particles in the γ matrix.

claim: The Ni-free sample at lower annealing temperatures (500 °C and 700 °C) formed α + needle-like κ-carbides instead of B2 stringer bands.
evidence_text: Te Ni-free followed the similar trend except that at lower annealing temperatures (e.g. 500°C and 700°C) where α + needle-like κ-carbides were observed instead of B2 stringer bands.

claim: In the Fe-15Mn-10Al-0.5C-5Ni alloy annealed at 500 °C, cuboidal κ precipitates are less than 100 nm in size.
Nano-indentation tests used a constant loading/unloading rate of 500 μN/s, a maximum load of 10000 μN, and a hold time of 20 s.
evidence_text: Nano-indentation tests were carried out at a constant loading/unloading rate of 500 μN s^{-1} up to a maximum load of 10000 μN with a hold time of 20s.

claim: Each measurement in a particular phase was repeated at least 25 times with arrays of 50–70 indentations applied across the region of interest.
evidence_text: Each measurement in a particular phase was repeated at least 25 times and in arrays of 50-70 indentations applied across the region of interest.

claim: Nano-hardness was measured for individual phases (solution-treated γ and α, B2 stringer bands, micro-sized and nano-disk B2) and for phase combinations: γ + cuboid κ, γ + cuboid κ + microsized B2, γ + microsized B2, α + needle-shape κ, and α + nano-disk B2.
evidence_text: Te measurements were performed for the following individual phases: solution-treated γ and α phases, B2 stringer bands, and both micro-meter sized and nano-disk B2. Te hardness of the following combinations of phases was also determined ...: γ + cuboid κ, γ + cuboid κ + microsized B2, γ + microsized B2, α + needle-shape κ, and α + nano-disk B2.

claim: Figure 4 shows load-depth curves and corresponding hardness values for bulk α and γ phases and composite bulk phases containing differently ordered precipitates.
evidence_text: Figure 4 shows the load-depth curves along with corresponding hardness values for the bulk α and γ phases (without precipitates) as well as the composite bulk phases containing differently ordered precipitates.
